title: A Beginner's Guide to Tracking Sources for Your Research Paper
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You find a great source. You copy the quote into your document. Three weeks later, you have no idea where that quote came from. Your bibliography is empty. Your deadline is tomorrow. Here's how to never let this happen again.

The One-Weekend Disaster

You spend a weekend collecting sources. You highlight great passages. You close the tabs. A week later, you have fifteen quotes and zero citations. You can't use any of them because you don't know where they came from. Don't be this person.

Track your sources from the moment you find them. Not later. Not 'when you have time.' Immediately.

Use a Spreadsheet (It's Free and Easy)

Create a simple spreadsheet with these columns:
• Source number (1, 2, 3...)
• Author name
• Article or book title
• Publication date
• URL or DOI
• Date you accessed it
• Key quotes (copy-paste)
• Your notes about why it's useful
• Page number (for books and PDFs)

One row per source. Fill it in as soon as you decide to use the source. This takes 30 seconds now and saves hours later.

Google Docs Add-ons That Do the Work for You

Install a citation manager like Zotero or Paperpile (both have free versions). They integrate with your browser. Click a button, and they save the source information automatically. When you're ready to write your bibliography, click another button, and they format it in MLA, APA, or Chicago style.

Zotero is free and open-source. Paperpile has a free trial for students. Both are worth learning.

Save the URL Immediately

For online sources, bookmark the page in a 'Research' folder. Or use a tool like Pocket to save it. But don't just bookmark — also copy the URL into your spreadsheet. URLs can break, but at least you'll have a record.

Pro tip: Use a URL shortener like TinyURL if the link is very long and ugly.

For Books: Take a Photo of the Copyright Page

When you check out a library book, take a photo of the copyright page. It has the publisher, publication date, and ISBN. You'll need this for your citation. If you return the book before finishing your paper, you'll still have the info.

Do this before you leave the library. Future you will be grateful.

Color-Code Your Sources by Claim

Use highlight colors to track which source supports which argument. Yellow for source A, blue for source B, pink for source C. Then when you're writing the paragraph about argument X, you know exactly which colored quotes to look for.

This works in both paper and digital documents.

The Golden Rule of Source Tracking

Never close a tab or return a book without first writing down the citation information. Never. Make this a non-negotiable rule. The moment you find a useful source, capture its information immediately.

A bibliography written in advance is a bibliography done. Do the boring work early, and the interesting writing part will be so much easier.
